topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The embossed letter felt like a physical insult in my hand. I stared at the carefully printed lines, the words about my academic prowess and future prospects suddenly tasting like ash. It was a complete fabrication. The scholarship, the one I’d poured every sleepless night into earning, was intended for someone else—a distant cousin, a placeholder. A cold, hollow sensation settled in my chest, tightening my throat until I could barely draw a breath. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound of the tearing sound unnervingly loud in the quiet library. I didn't move to throw it away; instead, I smoothed the creases meticulously, a strange, meticulous ritual of resentment taking over my hands. When Professor Albright called me in the next day, my voice was unnaturally level, a flat monotone that didn't betray the churning turmoil inside. "I see," I managed, meeting his eyes with a stare that held no warmth, only a deep, unyielding disappointment.

[story 2]

I traced the edges of the acceptance notification on my screen, the blue light reflecting off my suddenly clammy palms. Every line detailing my achievements seemed to mock me. It wasn't mine. It was a misdirection, a clerical error magnified into a personal slight. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran down my spine as I realized the depth of the deception. I pushed my chair back so forcefully it scraped against the linoleum floor, the sudden noise shattering the silence of the study room. I didn't cry. Instead, I leaned back, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, focusing intently on the grain of the wooden desk. "So, all that effort," I murmured to the empty room, my tone laced with a brittle edge, "was for a phantom." The air in the room felt thick, suffocating, as if the very atmosphere had been poisoned by this revelation.

[story 3]

He handed me the official documentation, his expression professionally neutral, which only amplified the sickening lurch in my stomach. The reality hit me like a physical blow: the entire structure of my academic future had been built on sand. A slow, deliberate movement took over me. I picked up the papers, my fingers trembling just enough to make the movement seem controlled, precise. I looked up, not at him, but past him, focusing on a distant point on the wall as if searching for an answer there. When I finally spoke, my voice was low, almost a whisper, but it carried a chilling finality. "This isn't right," I stated, the words devoid of any plea or anger, simply stating a fact that felt devastatingly unjust. I stood up, gathering my things with an economy of motion that spoke volumes about my present state of profound disillusionment.
